title: Mandelbrot set
content:
The Mandelbrot set () is the set of complex number s formula_1 for which the function formula_2 does not diverge when iterated from formula_3, i.e., for which the sequence formula_4, formula_5, etc., remains bounded in absolute value. Its definition is credited to Adrien Douady who named it in tribute to the mathematician Benoit Mandelbrot, a pioneer of fractal geometry. Images of the Mandelbrot set exhibit an elaborate and infinitely complicated boundary that reveals progressively ever-finer recursive detail at increasing magnifications, making the boundary of the Mandelbrot set a fractal curve.

The "style" of this repeating detail depends on the region of the set being examined. Mandelbrot set images may be created by sampling the complex numbers and testing, for each sample point formula_6 whether the sequence formula_7 goes to infinity. Treating the real and imaginary part s of formula_1 as image coordinate s on the complex plane, pixels may then be coloured according to how soon the sequence formula_9 crosses an arbitrarily chosen threshold (the threshold has to be at least 2, but is otherwise arbitrary).

If formula_1 is held constant and the initial value of formula_11 is varied instead, one obtains the corresponding Julia set for the point formula_1. The Mandelbrot set has become popular outside mathematics both for its aesthetic appeal and as an example of a complex structure arising from the application of simple rules. It is one of the best-known examples of mathematical visualization, mathematical beauty, and motif. History. The Mandelbrot set has its origin in complex dynamics, a field first investigated by the French mathematicians Pierre Fatou and Gaston Julia at the beginning of the 20th century.

This fractal was first defined and drawn in 1978 by Robert W. Brooks and Peter Matelski as part of a study of Kleinian group s. On 1 March 1980, at IBM's Thomas J. Watson Research Center in Yorktown Heights, New York, Benoit Mandelbrot first saw a visualization of the set. Mandelbrot studied the parameter space of quadratic polynomial s in an article that appeared in 1980.

The mathematical study of the Mandelbrot set really began with work by the mathematicians Adrien Douady and John H. Hubbard (1985), who established many of its fundamental properties and named the set in honor of Mandelbrot for his influential work in fractal geometry. The mathematicians Heinz-Otto Peitgen and Peter Richter became well known for promoting the set with photographs, books (1986), and an internationally touring exhibit of the German Goethe-Institut (1985). The cover article of the August 1985 "Scientific American" introduced a wide audience to the algorithm for computing the Mandelbrot set.

The cover featured an image located at −0.909 + −0.275 "i" and was created by Peitgen et al. The Mandelbrot set became prominent in the mid-1980s as a computer graphics demo, when personal computer s became powerful enough to plot and display the set in high resolution The work of Douady and Hubbard coincided with a huge increase in interest in complex dynamics and abstract mathematics, and the study of the Mandelbrot set has been a centerpiece of this field ever since.

An exhaustive list of all who have contributed to the understanding of this set since then is long but would include Jean-Christophe Yoccoz, Mitsuhiro Shishikura, Curt McMullen, John Milnor and Mikhail Lyubich. Formal definition. The Mandelbrot set is the set of values of "c" in the complex plane for which the orbit of the critical point "z" = 0 under iteration of the quadratic map remains bounded.

Thus, a complex number "c" is a member of the Mandelbrot set if, when starting with "z"0 = 0 and applying the iteration repeatedly, the absolute value of "z""n" remains bounded for all "n" 0.

For example, for "c" = 1, the <a href="sequence" sequence</a is 0, 1, 2, 5, 26,..., which tends to <a href="infinity" infinity</a, so 1 is not an element of the Mandelbrot set. On the other hand, for "c" = −1, the sequence is 0, −1, 0, −1, 0,..., which is bounded, so −1 does belong to the set.

The Mandelbrot set can also be defined as the <a href="connectedness%20locus" connectedness locus</a of a family of <a href="polynomial" polynomial</a s. Basic properties.

The Mandelbrot set is a <a href="compact%20set" compact set</a, since it is <a href="closed%20set" closed</a and contained in the <a href="closed%20disk" closed disk</a of radius 2 around the <a href="Origin%20%28mathematics%29" origin</a. More specifically, a point formula_1 belongs to the Mandelbrot set if and only if formula_15 for all formula_16.

In other words, the <a href="absolute%20value" absolute value</a of formula_17 must remain at or below 2 for formula_1 to be in the Mandelbrot set, formula_19, as if that absolute value exceeds 2, the sequence will escape to infinity. The <a href="intersection%20%28set%20theory%29" intersection</a of formula_19 with the real axis is precisely the interval [−2, 1/4].

The parameters along this interval can be put in one-to-one correspondence with those of the real <a href="logistic%20map" logistic family</a, The correspondence is given by In fact, this gives a correspondence between the entire <a href="parameter%20space" parameter space</a of the logistic family and that of the Mandelbrot set. Douady and Hubbard have shown that the Mandelbrot set is <a href="connected%20space" connected</a.

In fact, they constructed an explicit <a href="holomorphic%20function" conformal isomorphism</a between the complement of the Mandelbrot set and the complement of the <a href="closed%20unit%20disk" closed unit disk</a. Mandelbrot had originally conjectured that the Mandelbrot set is <a href="Disconnected%20%28topology%29" disconnected</a. This conjecture was based on computer pictures generated by programs that are unable to detect the thin filaments connecting different parts of formula_19.

Upon further experiments, he revised his conjecture, deciding that formula_19 should be connected. There also exists a <a href="Topology" topological</a proof to the connectedness that was discovered in 2001 by <a href="Jeremy%20Kahn" Jeremy Kahn</a.

The dynamical formula for the <a href="uniformization%20theorem" uniformisation</a of the complement of the Mandelbrot set, arising from Douady and Hubbard's proof of the connectedness of formula_19, gives rise to <a href="external%20ray" external ray</a s of the Mandelbrot set. These rays can be used to study the Mandelbrot set in combinatorial terms and form the backbone of the <a href="Jean-Christophe%20Yoccoz%23Mathematical%20work" Yoccoz parapuzzle</a.
